title: The Roll Of The Kettledrum; or, The Lay Of The Last Charger
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½You have the Pyrrhic dance as yet,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where is the Pyrrhic phalanx gone?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of two such lessons, why forget
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The nobler and the manlier one?ן¿½ 
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Byron.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One line of swart profiles and bearded lips dressing,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One ridge of bright helmets, one crest of fair plumes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One streak of blue sword-blades all bared for the fleshing,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One row of red nostrils that scent battle-fumes.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Forward! the trumpets were sounding the charge,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The roll of the kettledrum rapidly ran,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That music, like wild-fire spreading at large,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Maddenן¿½d the war-horse as well as the man.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Forward! still forward! we thunderן¿½d along,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Steadily yet, for our strength we were nursing;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tall Ewart, our sergeant, was humming a song,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lance-corporal Black Will was blaspheming and cursing.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Openן¿½d their volley of guns on our right,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Puffs of grey smoke, veiling gleams of red flame,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Curling to leeward, were seen on the height,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where the batteries were posted, as onward we came.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Spreading before us their cavalry lay,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Squadron on squadron, troop upon troop;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We were so few, and so many were they,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Eagles wait calmly the sparrow-hawkן¿½s stoop.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Forward! still forward! steed answering steed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Cheerily neighן¿½d, while the foam flakes were tossן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From bridle to bridle, the top of our speed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was gainן¿½d, but the pride of our order was lost.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One was there leading by nearly a rood,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though we were racing he kept to the fore,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Still as a rock in his stirrups he stood,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ High in the sunlight his sabre he bore.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Suddenly tottering, backwards he crashן¿½d,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Loudly his helm right in front of us rung;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iron hoofs thunderן¿½d, and naked steel flashן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Over him, youngest, where many were young.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now we were close to them, every horse striding
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Madly; St. Luce passן¿½d with never a groan;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sadly my master lookן¿½d round, he was riding
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the boyן¿½s right, with a line of his own.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thrusting his hand in his breast or breast-pocket,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While from his wrist the sword swung by a chain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Swiftly he drew out some trinket or locket,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Kissן¿½d it (I think) and replaced it again.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Burst, while his fingers reclosed on the haft,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Jarring concussion and earth shaking din,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Horse ן¿½counterן¿½d horse, and I reelן¿½d, but he laughן¿½d,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Down went his man, cloven clean to the chin!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wedged in the midst of that struggling mass,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ After the first shock, where each his foe singled,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Little was seen, save a dazzle, like glass
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the sun, with grey smoke and black dust intermingled.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Here and there reddenן¿½d a pistol shot, flashing
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Through the red sparkle of steel upon steel!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Redder the spark seemן¿½d, and louder the clashing,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Struck from the helm by the iron-shod heel!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Over fallen riders, like witherן¿½d leaves strewing
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Uplands in autumn, we sunderן¿½d their ranks;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Steeds rearing and plunging, men hacking and hewing,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fierce grinding of sword-blades, sharp goading of flanks.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Short was the crisis of conflict soon over,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Being too good (I suppose) to last long;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Through them we cut, as the scythe cuts the clover,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Batterן¿½d and stainן¿½d we emergן¿½d from their throng.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Some of our saddles were emptied, of course;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To heaven (or elsewhere) Black Will had been carried!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ned Sullivan mounted Willן¿½s riderless horse,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His mare being hurt, while ten seconds we tarried.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And then we re-formed, and went at them once more,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ere they had rightly closed up the old track,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We broke through the lane we had openן¿½d before,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And as we went forward eן¿½en so we came back.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Our numbers were few, and our loss far from small,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They could fight, and, besides, they were twenty to one;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We were clear of them all when we heard the recall,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And thus we returned, but my tale is not done.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For the hand of my rider felt strange on my bit,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He breathed once or twice like one partially choked,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And swayן¿½d in his seat, then I knew he was hit;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He must have bled fast, for my withers were soakן¿½d,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And scarcely an inch of my housing was dry;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I slackenן¿½d my speed, yet I never quite stoppן¿½d,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ere he patted my neck, said, ן¿½Old fellow, good-bye!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And droppן¿½d off me gently, and lay where he droppן¿½d!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ah, me! after all, they may call us dumb creatures,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I tried hard to neigh, but the sobs took my breath,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet I guessן¿½d gazing down at those still, quiet features,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He was never more happy in life than in death.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Two years back, at Aldershot, Elrington mentioned
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My name to our colonel one field-day. He said,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Countן¿½, ן¿½Steeltrapן¿½, and ן¿½Challengerן¿½ ought to be pensionן¿½d;ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Countן¿½ died the same week, and now ן¿½Steeltrapן¿½ is dead.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That morning our colonel was riding ן¿½Theresaן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The filly by ן¿½Teddingtonן¿½ out of ן¿½Mistakeן¿½;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His girls, pretty Alice and fair-haired Louisa,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Were there on the ponies he purchased from Blake.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I remember he pointed me out to his daughters,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Said he, ן¿½In this troop I may fairly take pride,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Iן¿½ve none left like him in my officersן¿½ quarters,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whose life-blood the mane of old ן¿½Challengerן¿½ dyed.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where are they? the war-steeds who shared in our glory,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The ן¿½Lanercostן¿½ colt, and the ן¿½Acrobatן¿½ mare,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the Irish division, ן¿½Kate Kearneyן¿½ and ן¿½Roryן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And rushing ן¿½Roscommonן¿½, and eager ן¿½Kildareן¿½,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ן¿½Freenyן¿½, a favourite once with my master,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ן¿½Warlockן¿½, a sluggard, but honest and true,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ן¿½Tancredן¿½, as honest as ן¿½Warlockן¿½, but faster,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ן¿½Blacklockן¿½, and ן¿½Birdlimeן¿½, and ן¿½Molly Carewן¿½?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All vanishן¿½d, what wonder! twelve summers have passן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Since then, and my comrade lies buried this day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Old ן¿½Steeltrapן¿½, the kicker, and now Iן¿½m the last
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the chargers who shared in that glorious fray.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Come, ן¿½Harlequinן¿½, keep your nose out of my manger,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Youן¿½ll get your allowance, my boy, and no more;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Snort! ן¿½Silvertailן¿½, snort! when youן¿½ve seen as much danger
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As I have, you wonן¿½t mind the rats in the straw.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Our gallant old colonel came limping and halting,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The day before yesterday, into my stall;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! light to the saddle Iן¿½ve once seen him vaulting,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In full marching order, steel broadsword and all.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And now his left leg than his right is made shorter
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Three inches, he stoops, and his chest is unsound;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He spoke to me gently, and patted my quarter,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I laid my ears back, and lookן¿½d playfully round.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For that word kindly meant, that caress kindly given,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I thankן¿½d him, though dumb, but my cheerfulness fled;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ More sadness I drew from the face of the living
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Than years back I did from the face of the dead.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For the dead face, upturnן¿½d, tranquil, joyous, and fearless,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lookן¿½d straight from green sod to blue fathomless sky
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With a smile; but the living face, gloomy and tearless,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And haggard and harassן¿½d, lookן¿½d down with a sigh.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Did he think on the first time he kissן¿½d Lady Mary?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the morning he wingן¿½d Horace Greville the beau?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the winner he steerן¿½d in the grand military?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the charge that he headed twelve long years ago?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Did he think on each fresh year, of fresh grief the herald?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On lids that are sunken, and locks that are grey?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On Alice, who bolted with Brian Fitzgerald?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On Rupert, his first-born, dishonourן¿½d by ן¿½playן¿½?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On Louey, his darling, who sleeps ן¿½neath the cypress,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That shades her and one whose last breath gave her life?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I saw those strong fingers hard over each eye press,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! the dead rest in peace when the quick toil in strife!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Scoff, man! egotistical, proud, unobservant,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Since I with manן¿½s grief dare to sympathise thus;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Why scoff? fellow-creature I am, fellow-servant
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of God, can man fathom Godן¿½s dealings with us?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The wide gulf that parts us may yet be no wider
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Than that which parts you from some being more blest;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And there may be more links ן¿½twixt the horse and his rider
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Than ever your shallow philosophy guessן¿½d.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You are proud of your power, and vain of your courage,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And your blood, Anglo-Saxon, or Norman, or Celt;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though your gifts you extol, and our gifts you disparage,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Your perils, your pleasures, your sorrows weן¿½ve felt.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We, too, sprung from mares of the prophet of Mecca,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And nursed on the pride that was born with the milk,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And filtered through ן¿½Crucifixן¿½, ן¿½Beeswingן¿½, ן¿½Rebeccaן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We love sheen of scarlet and shimmer of silk.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We, too, sprung from loins of the Ishmaelite stallions,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We glory in daring that dies or prevails;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From ן¿½counter of squadrons, and crash of battalions,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To rending of blackthorns, and rattle of rails.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In all strife where courage is tested, and power,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From the meet on the hill-side, the horn-blast, the find,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The burst, the long gallop that seems to devour
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The champaign, all obstacles flinging behind,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the cheer and the clarion, the war-music blended
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With war-cry, the furious dash at the foe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The terrible shock, the recoil, and the splendid
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bare sword, flashing blue, rising red from the blow.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ve borne ONE through perils where many have seen us,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No tyrant, a kind friend, a patient instructor,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Iן¿½ve felt some strange element flashing between us,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till the saddle seemן¿½d turnן¿½d to a lightning conductor.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Did he see? could he feel through the faintness, the numbness,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While lingerן¿½d the spirit half-loosed from the clay,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dumb eyes seeking his in their piteous dumbness,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dumb quivering nostrils, too stricken to neigh?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And what then? the colours reversed, the drums muffled,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The black nodding plumes, the dead march and the pall,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The stern faces, soldier-like, silent, unruffled,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The slow sacred music that floats over all!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Cross carbine and boar-spear, hang bugle and banner,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Spur, sabre, and snaffle, and helm, Is it well?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vain ן¿½scutcheon, false trophies of Mars and Diana,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Can the dead laurel sprout with the live immortelle?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It may be, we follow, and though we inherit
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Our strength for a season, our pride for a span,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Say! vanity are they? vexation of spirit?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not so, since they serve for a time horse and man.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They serve for a time, and they make life worth living,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In spite of lifeן¿½s troubles, ן¿½tis vain to despond;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, man! WE at least, WE enjoy, with thanksgiving,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Godן¿½s gifts on this earth, though we look not beyond.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You sin, and you suffer, and we, too, find sorrow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Perchance through your sin, yet it soon will be oן¿½er;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We labour to-day, and we slumber to-morrow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Strong horse and bold rider! and who knoweth more?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In our barrack-square shouted Drill-sergeant Mן¿½Cluskie,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The roll of the kettledrum rapidly ran,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The colonel wheelן¿½d short, speaking once, dry and husky,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Would to God I had died with your master, old man!ן¿½
meaning: About: Adam Lindsay Gordon was an Australian poet, jockey and politician.
